_source: Gen 2 Compendiums/CONSUMER MATHS - VOL 1 & 2 (TASC MTG215123, Module 2 Topic 3). Content grounded in the compendium; answer keys verified by tools/verify_answers.py.

unit.title: Consumer Arithmetic
unit.subtitle: Module 2 · Topic 3 · Finance
unit.description: Rates and percentages in the context of earning, spending and managing money: GST, mark-ups and discounts, exchange rates, budgeting, wages and salaries, simple and compound interest, and shares. Criteria C6 (finance) and C1 (communicate).

lessons.title: Percentages, GST, mark-ups & discounts
lessons.overview: The percentage toolkit that underpins all consumer arithmetic.

sections.title: Working with percentages
sections.html:
Three moves cover most percentage work:

  • One amount as a percentage of another: divide, then ×100. E.g. 18 out of 40 = 18÷40×100 = 45%.
  • A percentage of an amount: multiply by the percentage as a decimal. E.g. 15% of $240 = 0.15×240 = $36.
  • Increase or decrease by a percentage: multiply by (1 + rate) to increase or (1 − rate) to decrease. E.g. increase 80 by 25% = 80×1.25 = 100; a 25% discount on $840 = 840×0.75 = $630.

sections.title: Mark-ups and discounts
sections.html: A mark-up is a percentage increase a retailer adds to a cost price; a discount is a percentage decrease. Treat both as "increase/decrease by a percentage". A $60 item marked up 35% sells for 60×1.35 = $81. A $250 jacket at 30% off costs 250×0.70 = $175.

sections.title: GST
sections.html:
The Goods and Services Tax is a 10% tax on most goods and services.

  • Add GST: multiply the price by 1.10. A $52.50 item costs 52.50×1.10 = $57.75 including GST.
  • Find the GST inside an inclusive price: it is 1/11 of the inclusive price (because 10/110 = 1/11). The GST inside a $320 price is 320÷11 = $29.09, so the pre-GST price is $290.91.

lessons.title: Exchange rates & budgeting
lessons.overview: Converting currency and planning income against spending.

sections.title: Exchange rates
sections.html: An exchange rate tells you how much of a foreign currency you get for one Australian dollar. If 1 A$ = 1.09 NZ$, then A$450 converts to 450×1.09 = NZ$490.50. To go the other way, divide by the rate.

sections.title: Budgeting
sections.html:
A budget is a plan that matches expected income against expected spending over a period. List all income, list all expenditure, then compare:

  • Income > expenditure → a surplus.
  • Income < expenditure → a deficit (time to trim spending).

Expenditure items are either fixed (unavoidable, e.g. rent, insurance) or discretionary (optional, e.g. entertainment, takeaway). Cutting discretionary items is the usual way to remove a deficit.

lessons.title: Earning money: wages, salary & commission
lessons.overview: How pay is calculated from salaries, hourly rates, overtime and commission.

sections.title: Salary and wages
sections.html:
A salary is a fixed annual amount, usually paid in equal parts. Weekly pay = annual÷52; fortnightly = annual÷26; monthly = annual÷12. A $78,000 salary is 78000÷52 = $1500 per week.

A wage is paid by the hour. 38 hours at $24.50 = $931.

sections.title: Overtime
sections.html: Hours beyond the normal week are often paid at a higher rate: time-and-a-half (×1.5) or double time (×2). Working 38 normal hours at $22 plus 4 hours at time-and-a-half: 38×22 + 4×(22×1.5) = 836 + 132 = $968.

sections.title: Commission
sections.html: Commission pays a percentage of sales, sometimes on top of a fixed retainer. A salesperson on a $300 retainer plus 5% commission who sells $8000 of goods earns 300 + 0.05×8000 = $700.

lessons.title: Simple interest
lessons.overview: Interest calculated only on the original principal.

sections.title: The simple interest formula
sections.html:
When interest is calculated only on the original amount, it is simple (or flat) interest:

I = P × r × t

  • I = interest earned or charged
  • P = principal (amount invested or borrowed)
  • r = interest rate per annum, as a decimal (8% = 0.08)
  • t = time in years

sections.title: Worked example
sections.html:
$4200 invested at 12.5% p.a. flat for 18 months (1.5 years):

I = 4200 × 0.125 × 1.5 = $787.50. The account is then worth 4200 + 787.50 = $4987.50.

The formula can be rearranged to find r, t or P when the interest is known.

lessons.title: Compound interest
lessons.overview: Interest added to the principal each period, so it earns interest too.

sections.title: The compound interest formula
sections.html:
In practice interest is usually added to the principal each period, so the next period earns interest on a larger balance. This is compound interest:

A = P(1 + i)n

  • A = amount in the account after n periods
  • P = principal
  • i = interest rate per period, as a decimal
  • n = number of interest-bearing periods

The interest earned is A − P.

sections.title: Compounding more often than yearly
sections.html:
When interest is added quarterly, i is the annual rate ÷ 4 and n is the number of years × 4. E.g. 15% p.a. added quarterly means i = 0.0375 per quarter.

Worked example: $1000 at 12% p.a. compounded annually for 3 years: A = 1000×(1.12)3 = $1404.93 — compared with only $1360 under simple interest.

lessons.title: Shares & dividends
lessons.overview: Income from shares, and comparing share value.

sections.title: Dividends
sections.html:
A dividend is a share of company profit paid to shareholders.

Dividend paid = number of shares × dividend per share

500 shares paying 45c each returns 500×0.45 = $225.

sections.title: Comparing shares
sections.html:
Two ratios help compare shares (both use annual figures):

  • Dividend yield = (annual dividend per share ÷ share price) × 100%. A 60c dividend on a $12 share yields 0.60÷12×100 = 5%.
  • Price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio = share price ÷ earnings per share. A $12 share earning $0.80 per share has a P/E of 15.

Higher dividend yield and lower P/E are generally considered better value.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Percentages, GST & spending
quizzes.overview: Percentages, mark-ups, discounts, GST and exchange rates. Unlimited attempts.

questions.prompt: Express 18 as a percentage of 40.
Find 15% of $240.
A price of $80 is increased by 25%. What is the new price?
A jacket marked $840 is discounted 25%. Find the sale price.
A retailer marks up a $60 item by 35%. Find the selling price.
Find the total cost of a $52.50 item after 10% GST is added.
How much GST is contained in a GST-inclusive price of $320?
questions.explain: The GST is 1/11 of the inclusive price: 320 ÷ 11 = $29.09.

questions.prompt: Convert A$450 to New Zealand dollars if 1 A$ = 1.09 NZ$.
The GST rate in Australia is:
questions.explain: GST is a 10% tax on most goods and services.

questions.prompt: What single number do you multiply a price by to add 10% GST? (e.g. 1.05)

quizzes.title: Quiz: Earning & budgeting
quizzes.overview: Salaries, wages, overtime, commission and budgets. Unlimited attempts.

questions.prompt: A salary of $78,000 per year is paid weekly. Find the weekly pay.
Find the pay for 38 hours worked at $24.50 per hour.
A worker earns $22/hour for 38 hours plus 4 hours at time-and-a-half. Find the total pay.
A salesperson is paid 4% commission on sales of $12,000. Find the commission.
A retainer of $300 plus 5% commission on $8,000 of sales. Find the total earnings.
Weekly income is $1,500 and weekly spending is $1,320. Find the weekly surplus.
Which of these is a DISCRETIONARY budget item?
questions.explain: Discretionary items are optional. Rent, insurance and rates are fixed; a streaming subscription is discretionary.

questions.prompt: A budget where spending is greater than income is said to be in ______.
questions.explain: Spending greater than income is a deficit; income greater than spending is a surplus.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Interest, shares & dividends
quizzes.overview: Simple and compound interest, shares and dividends. Unlimited attempts.

questions.prompt: Find the simple interest on $4,200 at 12.5% p.a. for 18 months.
How much simple interest is charged on a $750 loan at 8% p.a. for 5 years?
$2,000 is invested at 6% p.a. simple interest for 3 years. Find the total amount in the account.
$1,000 is invested at 12% p.a. compounded annually for 3 years. Find the final amount.
$5,000 is invested at 15% p.a. compounded quarterly for 5 years. Find the final amount.
A shareholder owns 500 shares paying a dividend of 45c per share. Find the total dividend.
A share priced at $12 pays an annual dividend of 60c per share. Find the dividend yield as a percentage.
A share priced at $12 has earnings of $0.80 per share. Find the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio.
Which statement best describes compound interest?

choices: Interest is paid only on the original principal
Interest is added to the principal, so later interest is earned on a growing balance
Interest is never added to the account

questions.explain: With compound interest each period's interest is added to the principal, so the balance — and the interest it earns — grows.

questions.prompt: In the simple interest formula I = Prt, what does the letter P stand for? (one word)
questions.explain: P is the principal — the amount invested or borrowed.
